verses.text: So they brought the ark of God, and set it in the midst of the tent that David had pitched for it: and they offered burnt sacrifices and peace offerings before God. And when David had made an end of offering the burnt offerings and the peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of the LORD. And he dealt to every one of Israel, both man and woman, to every one a loaf of bread, and a good piece of flesh, and a flagon of wine. Guidance: The ark is set at last in the tent David prepared, sacrifices are offered, and David blesses the people in the name of the LORD and shares with every Israelite — man and woman alike — bread, meat, and wine. The great day of worship overflows into generosity and blessing toward the people. The chapter is showing us the fruit of God's presence rightly welcomed: worship, blessing, and open-handed joy. David does not hoard the gladness of the day but spreads it to all, giving to every person a portion of the feast. The chapter is teaching us that true worship of God issues in love and generosity toward others; having honoured God, David honours and provides for His people. The chapter is teaching us that the blessings of God are for 'every one of Israel, both man and woman' — none excluded, the humblest sharing in the feast as fully as the great. There is a beautiful inclusiveness in David's gift: every man and woman received the same portion, for all alike belonged to the LORD's people. The chapter is teaching us that those whom God has blessed should become channels of blessing, that worship and generosity belong together, and that the joy of the LORD's presence is meant to be shared widely and freely. As the king blessed the people 'in the name of the LORD,' so we are taught that all our blessing of others flows from God and points back to Him — and that a heart full of worship will be a heart full of open-handed love.

And he appointed certain of the Levites to minister before the ark of the LORD, and to record, and to thank and praise the LORD God of Israel: Asaph the chief, and next to him Zechariah, Jeiel, and Shemiramoth, and Jehiel, and Mattithiah, and Eliab, and Benaiah, and Obededom: and Jeiel with psalteries and with harps; but Asaph made a sound with cymbals; Benaiah also and Jahaziel the priests with trumpets continually before the ark of the covenant of God. Guidance: David appoints Levites, with Asaph as chief, 'to minister before the ark of the LORD, and to record, and to thank and praise the LORD God of Israel,' with priests sounding trumpets 'continually before the ark.' Here begins the ordered, ongoing ministry of praise that would mark Israel's worship from this day forward. The chapter is showing us the establishment of continual thanksgiving as a settled institution among God's people — not a one-day festival, but a daily, abiding ministry of praise before the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that thanksgiving and praise are to be a regular, appointed, ongoing part of the worship of God, not occasional bursts of feeling. David did not let the joy of the great day fade; he established men whose very calling was 'to thank and praise the LORD' continually. The chapter is teaching us the value of devoting ourselves, and appointing others, to the steady work of praise — that God should be thanked not only in high moments but every day, continually. The mention that the Levites were also 'to record' reminds us that remembering and writing down God's deeds is part of praise; we praise rightly when we keep the memory of His works alive. The chapter is teaching us to make thanksgiving a fixed habit and a holy calling, to keep the trumpets sounding 'continually before the ark' — for the God who dwells among His people is worthy of unceasing praise, and the heart that has known His presence will never run short of reasons to give thanks.

Then on that day David delivered first this psalm to thank the LORD into the hand of Asaph and his brethren. Give thanks unto the LORD, call upon his name, make known his deeds among the people. Sing unto him, sing psalms unto him, talk ye of all his wondrous works. Glory ye in his holy name: let the heart of them rejoice that seek the LORD. Seek the LORD and his strength, seek his face continually. Remember his marvellous works that he hath done, his wonders, and the judgments of his mouth; O ye seed of Israel his servant, ye children of Jacob, his chosen ones. Guidance: David hands to Asaph a psalm of thanksgiving — and its opening notes are a summons to seek and praise the LORD. 'Give thanks... call upon his name... make known his deeds.' 'Glory ye in his holy name: let the heart of them rejoice that seek the LORD. Seek the LORD and his strength, seek his face continually.' The chapter is showing us the very heart of the worshiping life: to seek the LORD continually and to rejoice in seeking Him. The chapter is teaching us that the proper occupation of God's people is to seek His face — not occasionally or in crisis only, but 'continually,' as the steady habit of the heart. And it sets before us the great promise that 'the heart of them rejoice that seek the LORD' — those who seek God find joy; the search itself is gladness, for He is found of them that seek Him. The chapter is teaching us to make known God's deeds, to talk of His wondrous works, to remember what He has done — for praise feeds on memory, and a thankful heart keeps God's marvellous works ever before it. The chapter is teaching us that we are 'his chosen ones,' the seed of His servant, and therefore have every reason to glory in His holy name. The whole burden of these verses is the very thing Saul failed to do and David lived to do: seek the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that the seeking heart is the rejoicing heart, and that to give thanks, to sing, to remember, and to seek His face continually is the joyful calling of all God's people. Here is the antidote to every barren and Michal-like soul: seek the LORD, and your heart shall rejoice.

He is the LORD our God; his judgments are in all the earth. Be ye mindful always of his covenant; the word which he commanded to a thousand generations; Even of the covenant which he made with Abraham, and of his oath unto Isaac; And hath confirmed the same to Jacob for a law, and to Israel for an everlasting covenant, Saying, Unto thee will I give the land of Canaan, the lot of your inheritance; When ye were but few, even a few, and strangers in it. And when they went from nation to nation, and from one kingdom to another people; He suffered no man to do them wrong: yea, he reproved kings for their sakes, Saying, Touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm. Guidance: The psalm turns to God's covenant faithfulness — His unbreakable promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, confirmed 'for an everlasting covenant,' to give them the land. And it recalls how the LORD protected the patriarchs when they 'were but few... and strangers,' wandering from place to place: 'He suffered no man to do them wrong: yea, he reproved kings for their sakes, saying, Touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm.' The chapter is showing us the LORD as the faithful Keeper of His covenant, who remembers His word 'to a thousand generations' and guards His people even when they are few and weak. The chapter is teaching us to 'be ye mindful always of his covenant' — to anchor our faith not in our own strength or numbers but in the promises God has sworn. For the post-exilic readers, few and surrounded by mighty empires, this was a word of great comfort: the same God who protected a handful of wandering patriarchs, who rebuked kings to keep them safe, was their God still. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are precious to Him and under His special protection — 'Touch not mine anointed' — so that no king or power can harm them but by His permission. The chapter is teaching us that God's covenant is everlasting and His care unfailing; He kept His word to the fathers across the centuries, and He keeps it still. When His people are few and feel like strangers in a hostile world, this is their stay: the covenant-keeping God watches over His own, and reproves the mighty for their sakes. Let us be ever mindful of His covenant, and rest in the faithfulness of Him who cannot lie.

And when they went from nation to nation, and from one kingdom to another people; He suffered no man to do them wrong: yea, he reproved kings for their sakes, Saying, Touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm. Guidance: The psalm turns to God's covenant faithfulness — His unbreakable promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, confirmed 'for an everlasting covenant,' to give them the land. And it recalls how the LORD protected the patriarchs when they 'were but few... and strangers,' wandering from place to place: 'He suffered no man to do them wrong: yea, he reproved kings for their sakes, saying, Touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm.' The chapter is showing us the LORD as the faithful Keeper of His covenant, who remembers His word 'to a thousand generations' and guards His people even when they are few and weak. The chapter is teaching us to 'be ye mindful always of his covenant' — to anchor our faith not in our own strength or numbers but in the promises God has sworn. For the post-exilic readers, few and surrounded by mighty empires, this was a word of great comfort: the same God who protected a handful of wandering patriarchs, who rebuked kings to keep them safe, was their God still. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are precious to Him and under His special protection — 'Touch not mine anointed' — so that no king or power can harm them but by His permission. The chapter is teaching us that God's covenant is everlasting and His care unfailing; He kept His word to the fathers across the centuries, and He keeps it still. When His people are few and feel like strangers in a hostile world, this is their stay: the covenant-keeping God watches over His own, and reproves the mighty for their sakes. Let us be ever mindful of His covenant, and rest in the faithfulness of Him who cannot lie.

Sing unto the LORD, all the earth; shew forth from day to day his salvation. Declare his glory among the heathen; his marvellous works among all nations. For great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised: he also is to be feared above all gods. For all the gods of the people are idols: but the LORD made the heavens. Glory and honour are in his presence; strength and gladness are in his place. Give unto the LORD, ye kindreds of the people, give unto the LORD glory and strength. Give unto the LORD the glory due unto his name: bring an offering, and come before him: worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness. Fear before him, all the earth: the world also shall be stable, that it be not moved. Guidance: The psalm now widens to embrace the whole earth: 'Sing unto the LORD, all the earth... Declare his glory among the heathen.' The God of Israel is not a local deity but the Maker of heaven, supreme above all so-called gods — 'for all the gods of the people are idols: but the LORD made the heavens.' Therefore all nations are summoned to give Him glory and to 'worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness.' The chapter is showing us the universal scope of God's glory and the missionary heart of true worship: the praise of God is not to be confined to Israel but proclaimed among all peoples. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD alone is God — the idols of the nations are nothing, mere lifeless things, while He made the very heavens; and so He alone is to be feared and praised. The chapter is teaching us to 'declare his glory among the heathen,' to long that all the earth should know and worship the true God — a vision fulfilled in the gospel that goes to all nations in the name of David's Son. The chapter is teaching us the right manner of worship: 'worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness,' bringing an offering, coming before Him with reverence and awe. There is a beauty in holy worship that the world's idolatry can never match. The chapter is teaching us to give God 'the glory due unto his name' — not a portion, but His full due — and to rejoice that 'strength and gladness are in his place.' The God who made the heavens, who is feared above all gods, is worthy of the worship of all the earth; let us join the universal chorus and call the nations to bow before Him in the beauty of holiness.

Let the heavens be glad, and let the earth rejoice: and let men say among the nations, The LORD reigneth. Let the sea roar, and the fulness thereof: let the fields rejoice, and all that is therein. Then shall the trees of the wood sing out at the presence of the LORD, because he cometh to judge the earth. O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever. And say ye, Save us, O God of our salvation, and gather us together, and deliver us from the heathen, that we may give thanks to thy holy name, and glory in thy praise. Blessed be the LORD God of Israel for ever and ever. And all the people said, Amen, and praised the LORD. Guidance: The psalm rises to its climax with all creation rejoicing in the reign of God: 'Let the heavens be glad, and let the earth rejoice: and let men say among the nations, The LORD reigneth.' The sea roars, the fields rejoice, the very trees of the wood sing out 'at the presence of the LORD, because he cometh to judge the earth.' Then comes the refrain that echoes through all Scripture: 'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever.' And a heartfelt prayer: 'Save us, O God of our salvation, and gather us together, and deliver us from the heathen' — closing with a great doxology to which all the people said 'Amen.' The chapter is showing us the joyful proclamation that 'the LORD reigneth' — the truth that sets the whole creation singing. The chapter is teaching us that God's kingship is cause for universal gladness; when men know that the LORD reigns and is coming to judge the earth in righteousness, even nature seems to rejoice. The chapter is teaching us the unfailing ground of all thanksgiving: 'he is good; his mercy endureth for ever' — His goodness and everlasting mercy are the bedrock of praise in every age. And the prayer 'Save us... gather us together, and deliver us' carried special weight for the post-exilic readers, scattered and oppressed: it is the cry of a people longing to be gathered and saved, that they might give thanks to God's holy name. The chapter is teaching us to long and pray for God's saving, gathering work, and to look forward to the day when 'the LORD reigneth' shall be the song of all the earth, when He comes to judge in righteousness, and His gathered people praise Him forever. 'Blessed be the LORD God of Israel for ever and ever' — and all the people said, Amen. So let us say our Amen, and praise the LORD.

So he left there before the ark of the covenant of the LORD Asaph and his brethren, to minister before the ark continually, as every day's work required: And Obededom with their brethren, threescore and eight; Obededom also the son of Jeduthun and Hosah to be porters: And Zadok the priest, and his brethren the priests, before the tabernacle of the LORD in the high place that was at Gibeon, To offer burnt offerings unto the LORD upon the altar of the burnt offering continually morning and evening, and to do according to all that is written in the law of the LORD, which he commanded Israel; And with them Heman and Jeduthun, and the rest that were chosen, who were expressed by name, to give thanks to the LORD, because his mercy endureth for ever; And with them Heman and Jeduthun with trumpets and cymbals for those that should make a sound, and with musical instruments of God. And the sons of Jeduthun were porters. And all the people departed every man to his house: and David returned to bless his house. Guidance: David carefully arranges the ongoing worship of God on two fronts: Asaph and his brethren to minister before the ark 'continually, as every day's work required' in Jerusalem, and Zadok with the priests at the tabernacle in Gibeon, to offer the burnt offerings 'continually morning and evening, and to do according to all that is written in the law of the LORD.' Heman and Jeduthun and the chosen singers were appointed 'to give thanks to the LORD, because his mercy endureth for ever.' Then, the great day done, 'David returned to bless his house.' The chapter is showing us David's diligence to establish worship that was both continual and according to the word — daily ministry before the ark, daily sacrifices morning and evening at the altar, all 'according to all that is written in the law.' The chapter is teaching us that the worship of God is to be both heartfelt and regulated by Scripture; David's joy did not make him careless of the law, but careful to do 'according to all that is written.' The chapter is teaching us the importance of daily, continual devotion — 'morning and evening,' 'every day's work,' the refrain 'his mercy endureth for ever' kept ever sounding. True worship is not a single great occasion but a steady, lifelong, daily ministry of praise and obedience. And the closing words are deeply instructive: after ordering the worship of the nation, 'David returned to bless his house.' The chapter is teaching us that the man who cares for the worship of God in public must also care for godliness in his own home; having blessed the people and provided for the praise of God, David goes home to bless his own household. Public devotion must not crowd out family religion. The chapter is teaching us to give ourselves to the continual worship of God according to His word, and never to neglect, amid all our service, the blessing of our own house.
